architecture.summary: A three-component self-correction system (Actor, Evaluator, Reflector) deployed on Qwen3-8B. The Actor generates reasoning trajectories; the Evaluator provides binary correctness signals. When a trajectory fails, the Reflector diagnoses errors by outputting a 5-class taxonomy (RETRIEVAL_FOCUS, BRIDGE_FAILURE, HALLUCINATION, INFERENCE_ERROR, FORMATTING_MISMATCH) plus a correction rule. The Reflector uses Outlines-based finite-state-machine constrained decoding to enforce strict JSON schema adherence at the logits level, guaranteeing 100% syntactic compliance without training. Diagnosed errors and corrections are appended to episodic memory, which the Actor reads on subsequent trials.

biblio.title: From Hallucination to Structure Snowballing: The Alignment Tax of Constrained Decoding in LLM Reflection

claims.statement: The application of logic-guided constrained decoding resulted in a marginal but observable performance penalty. The baseline accuracy of 50.0% dropped to 38.0% under constrained conditions.

Models that successfully maintained a correct status across both conditions (the TT group) consumed an average of 2,850 thinking tokens. In contrast, the degraded TF group exhibited a massive token consumption surge, averaging 4,005.5 tokens before ultimately failing.

claims.dataset: HotpotQA (filtered distractor setting, first-round reflection diagnoses)
claims.statement: Out of 100 first-round diagnoses, 96 categorised the failure as a FORMATTING_MISMATCH.

Simply imposing structural constraints does not improve self-correction performance. Instead, it triggers a new failure mode termed 'structure snowballing,' in which the model becomes trapped in formatting loops and fails to detect or resolve deeper semantic errors.

Strict constraints are not entirely detrimental; eleven samples successfully recovered from an incorrect baseline state to a correct state under constrained decoding (the FT group). However, twenty-three samples that the model answered correctly in the free-text baseline degraded to incorrect under structural constraints (the TF group).

When the decoding library heavily restricts the output vocabulary, the model shifts its attention weights toward syntactic compliance rather than semantic reasoning. The finite-state machine constraints force the model into 'formatting traps' and 'death loops,' where it achieves near-perfect superficial syntactic alignment yet fails to detect or resolve deeper semantic errors.

The cognitive load required to satisfy strict formatting rules pushes an 8-billion-parameter model into formatting traps. Unguided models default to shallow error correction; when forced into a structured taxonomy, the 8B parameter model lacks the cognitive capacity to dig for deep logical flaws but becomes exceptionally effective at identifying and patching surface-level syntax discrepancies.

These findings expose an 'alignment tax' inherent to constrained decoding, highlighting a tension between structural granularity and internal model capacity in autonomous workflows. Enforcing structured reflection via grammar-constrained decoding introduces an alignment tax when deployed without external training.
